questions.q: A container sets `requests.memory: 64Mi` and `limits.memory: 256Mi`. What QoS class is assigned?
questions.explanation: Burstable QoS is assigned when requests are set but differ from limits. The container requests 64Mi but can burst to 256Mi.

questions.q: Which QoS class is evicted FIRST when a node is under memory pressure?
questions.explanation: BestEffort pods have no resource requests or limits and are the first to be evicted under memory pressure. Guaranteed pods are evicted last.

questions.q: What happens when a container exceeds its memory limit?
options: It is throttled and slowed down
It is OOMKilled and the container is restarted
It is evicted from the node entirely
It is moved to a node with more memory
questions.explanation: When a container exceeds its memory limit, the Linux OOM killer terminates the process and the container is restarted (OOMKilled). Unlike CPU, memory cannot be throttled — exceeding the limit kills the process.

questions.q: A LimitRange sets `default.cpu: 500m` in namespace `dev`. A pod is created in `dev` without any resource spec. What CPU limit does the container get?
questions.explanation: The LimitRange's `default` field is automatically injected as the limit when a container doesn't specify one. So the container gets cpu limit: 500m.
1 CPU = 1000m (millicores). Therefore 0.5 CPU = 500m. Common values: 100m = 0.1 CPU, 500m = 0.5 CPU, 1000m = 1 CPU.

questions.q: What is the difference between LimitRange and ResourceQuota?
options: LimitRange applies to namespaces; ResourceQuota applies to containers
LimitRange sets per-container bounds and defaults; ResourceQuota caps total namespace consumption
They are the same but with different syntax
ResourceQuota injects defaults; LimitRange caps the total
questions.explanation: LimitRange controls individual container/pod resource bounds and injects defaults. ResourceQuota controls the total sum of resources consumed by all objects in a namespace.
